description:
Convert ReplayGain values to Apple's iTunNORM format so volume normalization works in Apple Music/iTunes.

## Background
amusic already calculates and writes ReplayGain tags. Apple SoundCheck uses a different format (iTunNORM) that Apple Music/iTunes reads for volume normalization. Converting between the two is a well-documented calculation.

## Scope
- Calculate iTunNORM values from existing ReplayGain track/album gain+peak values
- Write iTunNORM tag to files (MP3: ID3v2 comment frame, M4A: iTunes metadata atom)
- Add --apple-soundcheck flag to process/easy commands
- Option to generate from existing ReplayGain tags without recalculating
- Support both track and album normalization modes

## Value
Audiophiles with Apple ecosystems get proper volume normalization without re-scanning in iTunes. Natural companion to existing ReplayGain support.

## Notes
- Already listed as planned/TODO in the project
- taglib-wasm likely has getAppleSoundCheck()/setAppleSoundCheck() support
title: Apple SoundCheck generation

description:
Set up Homebrew distribution so users can install amusic via `brew install`.

## Background

The infrastructure is already in place in the repo:
- `homebrew/amusic.rb.template` — formula template with platform-specific binary URLs and SHA256 placeholders
- `.github/workflows/homebrew-update.yml` — GitHub Actions workflow that auto-generates a formula and opens a PR to the tap repo on each release
- `docs/RELEASING.md` — documents the full release flow including Homebrew updates

## Steps

### 1. Create the Homebrew Tap Repository
- Create a new **public** repo named `homebrew-tap` under the GitHub account
  ```
  gh repo create homebrew-tap --public --description "Homebrew tap for amusic"
  ```
- Initialize with a `Formula/` directory and a README
  ```
  mkdir -p Formula
  echo "# Homebrew Tap" > README.md
  git add . && git commit -m "Initial commit" && git push origin main
  ```

### 2. Create a Personal Access Token (PAT)
- Go to GitHub Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Tokens (classic)
- Create a token with `repo` scope
- Name it `HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN`

### 3. Add PAT to amusic Repository Secrets
- In the amusic repo: Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions
- Create secret named `HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN` with the token value

### 4. Verify the Workflow
- Create a test release to trigger `.github/workflows/homebrew-update.yml`
- Confirm it downloads checksums, generates the formula from template, and opens a PR to the tap repo
- Review and merge the PR

### 5. Test Installation
- `brew tap <username>/tap`
- `brew install amusic`
- Verify `amusic --version` matches the release
- Test on both macOS ARM64 and x86_64 if possible

### 6. Update README
- Add Homebrew installation instructions to the project README

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `homebrew-tap` repo exists and is public
- [ ] `HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN` secret is configured
- [ ] A release triggers automatic formula PR to the tap
- [ ] `brew install <username>/tap/amusic` works end-to-end
- [ ] README includes Homebrew install instructions
title: Publish amusic on Homebrew

close_reason: Implemented RG tag copy from source to encoded file after encoding succeeds in processTrack(). Added integration tests.
description:
When using `process --encode --replay-gain`, ReplayGain is calculated on the source directory but never applied to the encoded output files.

## Current Behavior

In `processAlbum` (src/lib/track_processor.ts:219-228):
1. ReplayGain is calculated on the **source album directory** via `rsgain`
2. Album gain data is stored in a Map keyed by source file paths
3. In `processTrack`, encoding runs first (step 1) and sets `workingPath` to the output file
4. ReplayGain step (step 2) checks `albumGainData` for the original `filePath` and sets `replayGainApplied = true` — but doesn't actually write tags to the encoded output

The result: encoded M4A files have **no ReplayGain tags**, even though the source files may have them.

## Expected Behavior

Encoded output files should have ReplayGain tags. Two viable approaches:

### Option A: Copy ReplayGain values to encoded files (recommended)
- After encoding, read ReplayGain values from source (or from `albumGainData` map)
- Write them to the encoded output via taglib-wasm
- Faster, no recalculation needed
- Values are equivalent for lossless→lossy transcodes (loudness doesn't change meaningfully)

### Option B: Run rsgain on output directory after all encoding completes
- More accurate for lossy→lossy transcodes (if --force-lossy-transcodes)
- Slower — requires full audio analysis of encoded files
- Requires restructuring the pipeline so encoding completes before ReplayGain

## Affected Code
- `src/lib/track_processor.ts` — `processTrack()` and `processAlbum()`
- `src/commands/process_collection.ts` — singles path also has `calculateGain: false`

## Notes
- Singles also skip ReplayGain entirely during encoding (line 114: `calculateGain: false`)
- The `rsgain` tool writes tags directly to source files, so source files DO get ReplayGain — it's only the encoded copies that are missing them
title: ReplayGain tags missing from encoded output files

description:
Find duplicate tracks across a library using AcoustID fingerprints, even when filenames and tags differ.

## Background
Music hoarders often have the same track multiple times from different sources — rips, downloads, compilations, greatest hits. AcoustID fingerprints identify the actual audio content regardless of metadata.

## Scope
- New `dupes` or `duplicates` subcommand
- Compare AcoustID fingerprints across all files in a library
- Group duplicates by fingerprint similarity (exact and near-matches)
- For each duplicate group, report:
  - File paths
  - Format and bitrate (quality comparison)
  - Tag completeness score
  - File size
  - Recommendation: which copy to keep (highest quality, best tags)
- Output modes:
  - Summary (default): duplicate groups with recommendation
  - Detailed (--verbose): full comparison per group
  - Machine-readable (--json)
- Optional: --delete-dupes flag with confirmation (dangerous, requires explicit opt-in)

## Value
Large libraries accumulate duplicates over years. Finding them manually is impractical. Fingerprint-based detection catches duplicates that filename/tag comparison misses.

## Dependencies
- Existing AcoustID fingerprint generation (src/lib/acoustid.ts)
- Requires fingerprints to be generated first (or generates on-the-fly)
title: Duplicate detection via AcoustID fingerprints

description: Implement tests for P0-P2 untested files: format.ts, replaygain.ts (parseReplayGainCSV), vendor_tools.ts, folder_operations.ts, show_tags_folder.ts (format helpers), and fast_discovery.ts (classifyDirectories)
title: Add tests for untested source files

description:
Batch-fix common tag inconsistencies: artist name variants, genre cleanup, junk in comment fields, unicode normalization.

## Background
Real-world music libraries have messy tags from different ripping software, download sources, and manual edits over years. Common problems include inconsistent artist names, genre tags that are all over the place, and comment fields full of ripper signatures.

## Scope
- New `normalize` or `clean` subcommand (or flags on existing commands)
- Artist normalization:
  - Detect variants of same artist ("AC/DC" vs "ACDC" vs "AC-DC")
  - Present groups for user confirmation before applying
  - Use MusicBrainz canonical names when available
- Genre cleanup:
  - Map common genre variants to canonical names
  - Remove junk genres ("Other", "Unknown", "(12)")
- Comment field cleanup:
  - Strip ripper/encoder signatures (e.g., "Ripped by...", "EAC V1.0...")
  - Optional: preserve user comments, only strip known junk patterns
- Unicode normalization:
  - NFC normalization for consistency
  - Fix mojibake (common in older MP3s)
- Dry-run by default, --apply to write changes
- Report showing all proposed changes before applying

## Value
The tedious cleanup nobody wants to do by hand. Makes libraries consistent for browsing and search. Pairs well with tag audit (fix what audit finds).
title: Tag normalization and cleanup

description:
Fetch and embed cover art from the Cover Art Archive using MusicBrainz release IDs.

## Background
MusicBrainz release IDs (available from AcoustID lookups) can be used to query the Cover Art Archive (coverartarchive.org), which hosts high-resolution album artwork.

## Scope
- Query Cover Art Archive API using MusicBrainz release IDs
- Download front cover art (primary use case)
- Embed artwork into audio files via taglib-wasm setPictures()
- Options:
  - Only add if missing (default)
  - Replace existing with higher resolution (--upgrade-art flag)
  - Preferred minimum resolution (e.g., --min-art-size 500)
- Support all formats taglib-wasm handles (MP3, M4A, FLAC, OGG, etc.)
- Dry-run support to preview what would change

## Value
Collectors care deeply about complete, high-quality cover art. Missing artwork is one of the most common complaints about downloaded/ripped libraries.

## Dependencies
- MusicBrainz metadata enrichment (amusic-xzl) for release IDs
- taglib-wasm getPictures()/setPictures() (already used for cover art copying)
title: Cover art fetching from Cover Art Archive

description:
Scan a library and report tagging problems: missing data, inconsistencies, and gaps.

## Background
Before fixing a library, users need to know what's wrong. A tag audit gives a clear picture of library health and prioritizes what to fix.

## Scope
- New `audit` or `lint` subcommand
- Checks per file:
  - Missing essential tags (title, artist, album, year, track number)
  - Missing cover art
  - Missing ReplayGain tags
  - Missing AcoustID fingerprint/ID
  - Suspiciously short or long durations
- Checks per album:
  - Inconsistent artist names within album
  - Mismatched years within album
  - Gaps in track numbering
  - Mixed formats within album
  - Missing disc number when multiple discs detected
- Output modes:
  - Summary report (default): counts by issue type
  - Detailed report (--verbose): per-file issues
  - Machine-readable (--json): for scripting
- Severity levels: error (missing title/artist), warning (missing year/genre), info (missing ReplayGain)

## Value
"Show me what's wrong" before "fix it all." Essential companion to MusicBrainz enrichment. Hoarders get a health check; audiophiles get a completeness report.
title: Tag audit/lint command

description:
Write International Standard Recording Codes (ISRC) and barcode/catalog numbers from MusicBrainz data into audio file tags.

## Background
ISRC codes uniquely identify recordings and are used by streaming services, rights organizations, and music databases. MusicBrainz stores ISRC data for many recordings. Barcodes/catalog numbers identify physical releases.

## Scope
- Fetch ISRC codes from MusicBrainz recording data (from AcoustID→MusicBrainz pipeline)
- Fetch barcode/catalog number from MusicBrainz release data
- Write ISRC to appropriate tag field per format:
  - MP3: TSRC frame (ID3v2)
  - FLAC/OGG: ISRC Vorbis comment
  - M4A: ----:com.apple.iTunes:ISRC
- Write barcode/catalog number to BARCODE/CATALOGNUMBER properties
- Only write if not already present (unless --force)
- Dry-run support

## Value
Niche but valued by completionists and collectors who want professional-grade metadata. Useful for identifying specific recordings/pressings.

## Dependencies
- MusicBrainz metadata enrichment (amusic-xzl) for recording/release IDs
title: ISRC and barcode embedding from MusicBrainz

description:
Extend encoding beyond macOS-only lossless→M4A to support more format targets and platforms.

## Background
Current encoding uses macOS afconvert (lossless → M4A/AAC only). This limits amusic to macOS for encoding and to a single output format.

## Scope
- Support additional output formats:
  - FLAC → Opus (excellent quality/size ratio, open format)
  - WAV → FLAC (lossless compression)
  - FLAC → MP3 (legacy compatibility)
- Cross-platform encoding via:
  - FFmpeg (if available, most capable)
  - Vendored opusenc/flac/lame binaries (consistent with vendor pattern)
  - Or: consider pure Wasm encoders for zero-dependency encoding
- Preserve all metadata and cover art during conversion
- Quality presets (e.g., --quality high/medium/low or --bitrate 128)
- Maintain existing afconvert path for macOS M4A (proven, fast)

## Value
Makes amusic useful for encoding on Linux/Windows. Opus support appeals to audiophiles who want modern efficient codecs. WAV→FLAC is table stakes for rippers.

## Considerations
- Vendoring multiple encoder binaries increases binary size significantly
- FFmpeg dependency vs. vendored single-purpose tools is a key design decision
- May want to make this a separate "encode-extended" or keep it under existing encode command
title: Expanded format conversion (cross-platform encoding)

description:
Use AcoustID fingerprint lookups (already implemented) to fetch rich metadata from MusicBrainz and offer to fix/fill tags.

## Background
amusic already looks up AcoustID fingerprints via the AcoustID API, which returns MusicBrainz recording IDs. This is the natural next step: use those IDs to fetch authoritative metadata.

## Scope
- Query MusicBrainz API using recording IDs returned from AcoustID lookups
- Fetch: correct title, artist, album, year, genre, track number, disc number, album artist
- Present diff of current vs. MusicBrainz metadata to user
- Offer to apply corrections (interactive or batch with --yes flag)
- Respect rate limits (MusicBrainz API: 1 req/sec with User-Agent)
- Handle ambiguous matches (multiple releases for same recording)

## Value
"Point it at a messy folder and get correct tags" — the killer feature for music hoarders. Audiophiles get authoritative metadata from the most trusted open music database.

## Dependencies
- Existing AcoustID pipeline (src/lib/acoustid.ts)
- taglib-wasm tag writing capabilities
title: MusicBrainz metadata enrichment
